original_hypothesis.title: Array Out-of-Bounds DoS in unstake Due to Stale Index After Eviction
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In StakingBase.sol, unstake calls checkpoint(), which may evict inactive services. The _evict function removes services by swapping them with the last element in setServiceIds and popping. However, unstake determines the index (idx) of the unstaking service from a COPY of the array taken BEFORE evictions occur. If another service is evicted during the checkpoint, and the unstaking service happens to be at the end of the array, _evict will move the unstaking service and reduce the array length. When unstake attempts to remove the service using its stale idx, it accesses an out-of-bounds index, causing the transaction to revert and permanently locking the user's service NFT and rewards.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: Using an index derived from a pre-mutation array copy to mutate the post-mutation array leads to out-of-bounds access or incorrect element removal.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setServiceIds[idx] = setServiceIds[setServiceIds.length - 1]; uses stale idx from before checkpoint()

preconditions: User calls unstake on a service located at the end of setServiceIds
Another service in the array is evicted during the internal checkpoint() call

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker can intentionally trigger evictions to DoS other users' unstake transactions
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack; locks victim's NFT and accumulated rewards

economic_questions: Can an attacker reliably trigger this to lock specific high-value services?
validation_requirements: Simulate unstake of the last array element while another element is eligible for eviction

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (cost to stake a dummy service and let it become inactive)

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Loss of user funds and NFTs

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: 100% loss of staked NFT and pending rewards
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Value of staked NFTs and accumulated rewards

validation_result.impact_summary: When unstake() triggers checkpoint() that evicts services, the index used to remove the unstaking service from setServiceIds is derived from a pre-eviction snapshot. If evictions shrink the array, the stale index causes an out-of-bounds revert, creating a DoS on unstake. Not permanently locked because calling checkpoint() separately first works around it.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The unstake() function calls checkpoint(), which returns a COPY of setServiceIds taken before evictions occur. Evictions modify the actual setServiceIds via swap-and-pop in _evict(). The unstake function then searches the stale copy for the service's index and uses that index to modify the real setServiceIds. If evictions removed services at indices lower than the unstaking service, the array has shrunk but the stale index hasn't changed, causing an out-of-bounds access.

Concrete scenario: setServiceIds = [A, B]. User calls unstake(B). checkpoint() runs: _calculateStakingRewards() captures serviceIds = [A, B]. Service A is inactive and evicted: _evict swaps index 0 with last (index 1) and pops, leaving setServiceIds = [B] (length 1). Back in unstake, the stale serviceIds = [A, B] finds B at idx=1. Then setServiceIds[1] is accessed, but setServiceIds.length is now 1, causing a revert.

Mitigation exists: calling checkpoint() as a standalone call before unstake(). When unstake later triggers checkpoint and it doesn't execute (not enough time passed), getServiceIds() is used instead, providing the correct current state. However, this workaround is not documented and users would face unexpected reverts.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is correct. Lines 557-560 of StakingBase.sol use idx derived from the stale serviceIds copy to index into the mutated setServiceIds array. The _evict function at lines ~361-389 modifies setServiceIds via swap-and-pop during checkpoint, and the serviceIds returned by checkpoint (from _calculateStakingRewards at lines ~404-449) is a snapshot taken before evictions.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: idx is computed from the stale serviceIds array (snapshot before evictions). After _evict modifies setServiceIds, idx can exceed setServiceIds.length, causing an out-of-bounds revert.
_evict modifies setServiceIds during checkpoint, reducing its length. The returned serviceIds from checkpoint is a pre-eviction snapshot, creating index mismatch.
serviceIds is a copy of setServiceIds taken before any evictions happen. This copy is returned by checkpoint() and used by unstake() to determine idx.

calling_sequence: 3. checkpoint calls _calculateStakingRewards() which creates serviceIds as a COPY of setServiceIds (e.g., [A, B])
4. checkpoint identifies service A as inactive beyond maxInactivityDuration, marks it for eviction
5. checkpoint calls _evict() which swaps A's position with last element and pops: setServiceIds becomes [B] (length 1)
6. checkpoint returns serviceIds=[A, B] (stale copy) and evictServiceIds=[A, 0]
7. unstake loops through stale serviceIds, finds B at idx=1
8. unstake attempts setServiceIds[1] but setServiceIds.length==1, causing out-of-bounds revert
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The vulnerability is in the disconnect between the serviceIds snapshot returned by checkpoint() and the actual setServiceIds state after evictions. checkpoint() -> _calculateStakingRewards() creates the snapshot at the start, then _evict() modifies the real setServiceIds. unstake() uses the snapshot's indices to access the modified array.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: An attacker stakes a service and deliberately makes it inactive (doesn't submit multisig transactions). After enough inactivity accumulates (> maxInactivityDuration), any time a victim tries to unstake a service that appears later in the setServiceIds array, the attacker's service gets evicted during checkpoint, shrinking the array, and the victim's unstake reverts. The victim must separately call checkpoint() first, then unstake. This requires knowledge of the workaround. The attack cost is one staking deposit for the dummy inactive service.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The impact is a conditional DoS on unstake(). When checkpoint triggers evictions, users whose services are at higher indices than evicted services cannot unstake in the same transaction. The workaround is to call checkpoint() separately first. This makes the bug a medium-severity DoS rather than a permanent fund lock. However, it violates the expected atomicity of unstake() and can cause confusing reverts for users. If multiple inactive services exist, the condition could repeatedly occur.
recommended_mitigations: After checkpoint() returns, re-read setServiceIds to determine the correct index of the unstaking service instead of using the stale snapshot
Alternative: search the actual setServiceIds array directly for the serviceId instead of using the returned serviceIds copy
Example fix: replace the idx search loop with: for (uint256 i = 0; i < setServiceIds.length; ++i) { if (setServiceIds[i] == serviceId) { idx = i; inSet = true; break; } }
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy StakingNativeToken via StakingFactory with maxNumServices=3, maxNumInactivityPeriods=1, livenessPeriod=100s. 2. Stake services A and B. 3. Advance time past livenessPeriod without service A performing any multisig txns (A will be inactive). 4. Advance time further until A's inactivity > maxInactivityDuration. 5. Call unstake(B). 6. Verify that the transaction reverts due to out-of-bounds array access when A is at a lower index than B. 7. Verify that calling checkpoint() separately first, then unstake(B), succeeds.
uncertainties: The exact line numbers in the proof_of_signal (557-560) may vary depending on compiler version or formatting; the logic pattern is correct regardless
While the bug is confirmed, permanent fund lock is unlikely since the workaround of calling checkpoint() separately exists
additional_testing_needed: Test with multiple simultaneous evictions to verify all edge cases
Test with the unstaking service being swapped during eviction (i.e., unstaking service at the end, a service earlier gets evicted and the unstaking service moves)
Verify the workaround (separate checkpoint call) works reliably in all scenarios
